model.description: Anthropic • Fast flagship Claude model for coding, analysis, and high-throughput agent use.

pick.rationale: Duke enters the Sweet 16 as the consensus #1 team in the country (KenPom #1, Torvik #1, AdjEM +39.17) with a roughly 14-point efficiency advantage over St. John's (KenPom #20, AdjEM +24.80), anchored by the nation's best defense (AdjDE 88.7) that is perfectly positioned to neutralize a Red Storm offense ranked only 47th nationally. The loss of starting point guard Caleb Foster (fractured foot, out indefinitely) is a genuine concern that trims confidence — Duke's ACC Tournament games without him were closer than expected — but the Blue Devils still won the conference title on an 11-game winning streak with Cameron Boozer anchoring an elite two-way roster. St. John's elite defense (12th nationally) under Rick Pitino is a legitimate weapon and gives the Red Storm a credible upset path, but their offense ranked 47th nationally runs into the toughest defensive assignment they have faced all season. The ~14-point efficiency gap, Duke's superior roster talent, and the historical dominance of top-ranked programs in the Sweet 16 make the Blue Devils a clear pick despite the Foster-shaped asterisk.

reasoningStep.title: Duke vs. St. John's Sweet 16 — Holistic Matchup Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Duke is the overwhelming analytical favorite at KenPom #1 / Torvik #1 with a ~14-point AdjEM edge over St. John's. The key risk factor is Caleb Foster's fractured foot (out indefinitely), which depletes Duke's backcourt depth, but the Blue Devils won the ACC Tournament without him on an 11-game winning streak and Patrick Ngongba was trending toward availability. St. John's is a legitimate Sweet 16 team — 12th nationally in defense, Big East Tournament champions — but their offense (47th nationally) runs into the nation's best defense, and the efficiency gap is too large to overcome in single-elimination play.
evidence: Duke: KenPom #1 AdjEM +39.17, AdjOE 127.9 (4th nationally), AdjDE 88.7 (1st nationally). St. John's: KenPom #20 AdjEM +24.80, AdjOE 119.9 (46th), AdjDE 95.1 (12th). Efficiency gap: ~14.4 KenPom points.
Caleb Foster (starting PG) out indefinitely with fractured foot per Jon Scheyer — 'a little while still.' Patrick Ngongba was day-to-day entering tournament, trending toward return for R64.
Duke won ACC Tournament without Foster on an 11-game winning streak, capping with a 74-70 win over Virginia, demonstrating sufficient roster depth to absorb the loss.
St. John's beat Kansas in R32 aided by Kansas injuries (McCullar out, Dickinson playing hurt). Red Storm arrive fully healthy with Rick Pitino's elite defensive system intact.
Duke listed as overall tournament favorite at +300-325 in betting markets, reflecting broad consensus on their superiority despite the Foster absence.

result.text: Jon Scheyer updates status of Caleb Foster, Patrick Ngongba Skip to main content Home Schedule Football Basketball Recruiting Odds Newsletter College Sports Wire USA TODAY Sports Jon Scheyer updates status of Caleb Foster, Patrick Ngongba Brian Stultz Duke Wire Duke has won 11 straight games entering the NCAA Tournament, including running through the ACC tourney for its second straight title, capped with a 74-70 victory over Virginia on Saturday, despite injuries to two starters: Caleb Foster and Patrick Ngongba. As the Blue Devils start the NCAA Tournament on Thursday against Siena as the top overall seed, Jon Scheyer gave an update on the status of the two players. "Caleb is going to be a little while still," Scheyer said on Sunday. "Again, he's progressing well.” Foster fractured his foot in the final regular-season game against North Carolina and has been out since. Still, his impact on the bench continues to help the Blue Devils. "Caleb, I've never seen a guy shoot so much in a scooter," Scheyer said. "... Multiple times a game, I have to ask him to shut up for one second just to get in a few coaching points. While Foster is out for some time, the news seems better on Ngongba’s potential return. "As far as Pat goes, I think he's been doing his therapy, working with the medical team,” Scheyer said. “They're just benchmarks. He just has to continue to take it – it's a day-by-day thing to see how he can handle the next step and whether that soreness goes away. I can tell you he's improving. I feel very positive about it, but I can't say what that means as far as later in the week in terms of timing or readiness, because we have to take it step by step." result.text: 2026 NCAA Tournament Quick Analysis: East And West Regions | OutKick OutKick Sports Culture Analysis Watch Shows Shop Newsletters Search Home Sports OutKick's 2026 NCAA Tournam… OutKick's 2026 NCAA Tournament Breakdown: East And West Regions Duke and Arizona headline two loaded regions packed with NBA talent and danger games. Dan Zaksheske Published March 15, 2026 8:00 PM EDT • Updated March 15, 2026 7:58 PM EDT Facebook Twitter Email Copy Link After months of regular-season action, a thrilling two weeks of conference championship play and one important bracket reveal, the 2026 NCAA Tournament is officially here. OutKick has you covered with a complete breakdown of the bracket , with the information you need to know for the East and West regions below.  If you're looking for the Midwest and South regions, we've got those breakdowns as well .  Learn More About The Ultimate College Hoops Experience East Region No. 1 Seed – Duke (32-2) Why they can make a run to the Final Four:  Because Duke is the best team in the country with one of the best players, Cameron Boozer, who is a likely Top 3 pick in the 2026 NBA Draft. Duke should be favored to cut down the nets at the end of the NCAA Tournament, and they're the top-rated team by analytics guru Ken Pomeroy .  What might hold them back in the NCAA Tournament:  The East region. Duke drew a very difficult road to the Final Four with a potential matchup against either Kansas or St. John's in the Sweet 16 and either UConn or Michigan State in the Elite Eight.  Duke Blue Devils forward Cameron Boozer celebrates after winning the ACC Conference Tournament Championship. (Bob Donnan/Imagn Images) No. 2 Seed – UConn (29-5) Why they can make a run to the Final Four:  This is an incredibly talented squad with a Top 15 defense. They have some of the most impressive wins of any team in the country, including early-season victories over Florida, Kansas, Illinois, and BYU. Plus, they had a heartbreaking loss to Arizona (71-67) in a game the Huskies led in the final two minutes.  What might hold them back in the NCAA Tournament:  The selection committee decided to keep the Huskies on the two-line despite an embarrassing 20-point loss in the Big East championship against St. John's. Connecticut has been incredibly inconsistent this season and if "bad UConn" shows up early, they might not make it very far.  No. 3 Seed – Michigan St. (25-7) Why they can make a run to the Final Four:  The Spartans ran the Big Ten gauntlet and had a 15-5 record in Big Ten play, showing the team can compete against some of the best teams in college basketball. They're an experienced squad with multiple upperclassmen contributing big minutes for Tom Izzo.  What might hold them back in the NCAA Tournament:  They have no momentum heading into the Big Dance. Sparty lost its regular season finale against Michigan and then suffered

result.text: Jon Scheyer updates status of Caleb Foster, Patrick Ngongba Skip to main content Home Schedule Football Basketball Recruiting Odds Newsletter College Sports Wire USA TODAY Sports Jon Scheyer updates status of Caleb Foster, Patrick Ngongba Brian Stultz Duke Wire Duke has won 11 straight games entering the NCAA Tournament, including running through the ACC tourney for its second straight title, capped with a 74-70 victory over Virginia on Saturday, despite injuries to two starters: Caleb Foster and Patrick Ngongba. As the Blue Devils start the NCAA Tournament on Thursday against Siena as the top overall seed, Jon Scheyer gave an update on the status of the two players. "Caleb is going to be a little while still," Scheyer said on Sunday. "Again, he's progressing well.” Foster fractured his foot in the final regular-season game against North Carolina and has been out since. Still, his impact on the bench continues to help the Blue Devils. "Caleb, I've never seen a guy shoot so much in a scooter," Scheyer said. "... Multiple times a game, I have to ask him to shut up for one second just to get in a few coaching points. While Foster is out for some time, the news seems better on Ngongba’s potential return. "As far as Pat goes, I think he's been doing his therapy, working with the medical team,” Scheyer said. “They're just benchmarks. He just has to continue to take it – it's a day-by-day thing to see how he can handle the next step and whether that soreness goes away. I can tell you he's improving. I feel very positive about it, but I can't say what that means as far as later in the week in terms of timing or readiness, because we have to take it step by step." Facebook Twitter Email Help Terms of Service Subscription Terms & Conditions Privacy Policy Site Map Accessibility Our Ethical Principles Responsible Disclosure Your Privacy Choices © Copyright Gannett 2026

result.text: 2026 NCAA Tournament Quick Analysis: East And West Regions | OutKick OutKick Sports Culture Analysis Watch Shows Shop Newsletters Search Home Sports OutKick's 2026 NCAA Tournam… OutKick's 2026 NCAA Tournament Breakdown: East And West Regions Duke and Arizona headline two loaded regions packed with NBA talent and danger games. Dan Zaksheske Published March 15, 2026 8:00 PM EDT • Updated March 15, 2026 7:58 PM EDT Facebook Twitter Email Copy Link After months of regular-season action, a thrilling two weeks of conference championship play and one important bracket reveal, the 2026 NCAA Tournament is officially here. OutKick has you covered with a complete breakdown of the bracket , with the information you need to know for the East and West regions below.  If you're looking for the Midwest and South regions, we've got those breakdowns as well .  Learn More About The Ultimate College Hoops Experience East Region No. 1 Seed – Duke (32-2) Why they can make a run to the Final Four:  Because Duke is the best team in the country with one of the best players, Cameron Boozer, who is a likely Top 3 pick in the 2026 NBA Draft. Duke should be favored to cut down the nets at the end of the NCAA Tournament, and they're the top-rated team by analytics guru Ken Pomeroy .  What might hold them back in the NCAA Tournament:  The East region. Duke drew a very difficult road to the Final Four with a potential matchup against either Kansas or St. John's in the Sweet 16 and either UConn or Michigan State in the Elite Eight.  Duke Blue Devils forward Cameron Boozer celebrates after winning the ACC Conference Tournament Championship. (Bob Donnan/Imagn Images) No. 2 Seed – UConn (29-5) Why they can make a run to the Final Four:  This is an incredibly talented squad with a Top 15 defense. They have some of the most impressive wins of any team in the country, including early-season victories over Florida, Kansas, Illinois, and BYU. Plus, they had a heartbreaking loss to Arizona (71-67) in a game the Huskies led in the final two minutes.  What might hold them back in the NCAA Tournament:  The selection committee decided to keep the Huskies on the two-line despite an embarrassing 20-point loss in the Big East championship against St. John's. Connecticut has been incredibly inconsistent this season and if "bad UConn" shows up early, they might not make it very far.  No. 3 Seed – Michigan St. (25-7) Why they can make a run to the Final Four:  The Spartans ran the Big Ten gauntlet and had a 15-5 record in Big Ten play, showing the team can compete against some of the best teams in college basketball. They're an experienced squad with multiple upperclassmen contributing big minutes for Tom Izzo.  What might hold them back in the NCAA Tournament:  They have no momentum heading into the Big Dance. Sparty lost its regular season finale against Michigan and then suffered

modelTrace.content: {"pick":{"gameId":"s16-east-1","winnerId":"duke","confidence":0.82,"rationale":"Duke enters the Sweet 16 as the consensus #1 team in the country (KenPom #1, Torvik #1, AdjEM +39.17) with a roughly 14-point efficiency advantage over St. John's (KenPom #20, AdjEM +24.80), anchored by the nation's best defense (AdjDE 88.7) that is perfectly positioned to neutralize a Red Storm offense ranked only 47th nationally. The loss of starting point guard Caleb Foster (fractured foot, out indefinitely) is a genuine concern that trims confidence — Duke's ACC Tournament games without him were closer than expected — but the Blue Devils still won the conference title on an 11-game winning streak with Cameron Boozer anchoring an elite two-way roster. St. John's elite defense (12th nationally) under Rick Pitino is a legitimate weapon and gives the Red Storm a credible upset path, but their offense ranked 47th nationally runs into the toughest defensive assignment they have faced all season. The ~14-point efficiency gap, Duke's superior roster talent, and the historical dominance of top-ranked programs in the Sweet 16 make the Blue Devils a clear pick despite the Foster-shaped asterisk."},"reasoningStep":{"id":"s16-east-1-analysis","title":"Duke vs. St. John's Sweet 16 — Holistic Matchup Analysis","summary":"Duke is the overwhelming analytical favorite at KenPom #1 / Torvik #1 with a ~14-point AdjEM edge over St. John's. The key risk factor is Caleb Foster's fractured foot (out indefinitely), which depletes Duke's backcourt depth, but the Blue Devils won the ACC Tournament without him on an 11-game winning streak and Patrick Ngongba was trending toward availability. St. John's is a legitimate Sweet 16 team — 12th nationally in defense, Big East Tournament champions — but their offense (47th nationally) runs into the nation's best defense, and the efficiency gap is too large to overcome in single-elimination play.","evidence":["Duke: KenPom #1 AdjEM +39.17, AdjOE 127.9 (4th nationally), AdjDE 88.7 (1st nationally).
